rulings.Case Title: LOGICAL IDEAS, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. COASTAL VENTURES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Issue Sanctions And Terminating Sanctions Against Coastal Ventures, Inc. Dba Mediatoo For Misuse Of The Discovery Process; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 30, 2018, Line 2, PLAINTIFF LOGICAL IDEAS, INC.'s, Motion For Issue Sanctions And Terminating Sanctions Against Coastal Ventures, Inc. Dba Mediatoo For Misuse Of The Discovery Process.(part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted to the extent that the Answer of defendant Coastal Ventures, Inc., be stricken. The motion is otherwise denied. Issue sanctions are unnecessary as against defendant Coastal Ventures, Inc., since its Answer has been stricken and monetary sanctions for its discovery abuse have already been awarded on the motion to compel its attendance at deposition. In addition, the issue sanctions requested could not be ordered as framed because the same would amount to a finding of partnership liability against defendant James Olsen (sued as "Jamie Olsen") in the amount of approximately $745,885.20. Defendant Olsen was not given notice as a subject of the discovery motion and no claim of abuse of the discovery process by him has been asserted or proven. Moreover, defendant Olsen has consistently denied any role as a principal or partner in any business transaction with plaintiff. In addition, the Court finds that plaintiff's counsel did not meet and confer with counsel to defendant Olsen in a good faith effort to resolve the discovery dispute created by plaintiff's disingenuous attempt to secure liability findings against Olsen based on discovery failures on the part of Chiang and Coastal Ventures, Inc. Under the circumstances, the Court finds plaintiff's request for issue sanctions against a non-noticed party to the discovery motion, coupled with its refusal to withdraw said request during the meet and confer process, to be abuses of the discovery process. The Court further finds that said abuses unnecessarily caused defendant Olsen incur $2,360.00 in legal expenses. The Court has already awarded sanctions in said amount in favor of defendant Olsen and against plaintiff and its attorneys based on the identical sanctions motion as to defendant Chiang. Therefore, no sanctions are awarded on this motion. Counsel to plaintiff shall submit a proposed order in Word format consistent with the foregoing by email to jameshfleming@gmail.com prior to the hearing.(end of part 1 of 2)=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LOGICAL IDEAS, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. COASTAL VENTURES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Issue Sanctions And Terminating Sanctions Against Richard Chiang For Misuse Of The Discovery Process; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 30, 2018, Line 2, PLAINTIFF LOGICAL IDEAS, INC.'s, Motion For Issue Sanctions And Terminating Sanctions Against Richard Chiang For Misuse Of The Discovery Process.(part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted to the extent that the Answer of defendant Chiang shall be stricken. The motion is otherwise denied. Issue sanctions are unnecessary as against defendant Chiang since his Answer has been stricken and monetary sanctions for his discovery abuse have already been awarded on the motion to compel Chiang's attendance at deposition. In addition, the issue sanctions requested could not be ordered as framed because the same would amount to a finding of partnership liability against defendant James Olsen (sued as "Jamie Olsen") in the amount of approximately $745,885.20. Defendant Olsen was not given notice as a subject of the discovery motion and no claim of abuse of the discovery process by him has been asserted or proven. Moreover, defendant Olsen has consistently denied any role as a principal or partner in any business transaction with plaintiff. In addition, the Court finds that plaintiff's counsel did not meet and confer with counsel to defendant Olsen in a good faith effort to resolve the discovery dispute created by plaintiff's disingenuous attempt to secure liability findings against Olsen based on discovery failures on the part of Chiang and Coastal Ventures, Inc. Under the circumstances, the Court finds plaintiff's request for issue sanctions against a non-noticed party to the discovery motion, coupled with its refusal to withdraw said request during the meet and confer process, to be abuses of the discovery process. The Court further finds that said abuses unnecessarily caused defendant Olsen incur $2,360.00 in legal expenses. Therefore, the Court awards sanctions of $2,360.00 in favor of defendant Olsen and against plaintiff and its attorneys. Said sum shall be paid on or before June 8, 2018. Counsel to plaintiff shall submit a proposed order in Word format consistent with the foregoing by email to jameshfleming@gmail.com prior to the hearing. (end of part 1 of 2)=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: BRUCE SHANNON VS. U.S. HEALTHWORKS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Pay Transfer Fees
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 30, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANTS DON SIMPSON, U.S. HEALTHWORKS MEDICAL GROUP'S Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Pay Transfer Fees. Defendants U.S. Healthworks Medical Group and Don Simpson's motion to dismiss for failure to pay transfer fees is granted. No opposition filed. Per CCP 339, this case is dismissed without prejudice due to the failure of plaintiff Bruce Shannon to pay transfer fees and no other lawsuit may be commenced in another court on the claims alleged by Mr. Shannon unless and until he pays the order requiring him to pay transfer fees. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: DAVID KANE ET AL VS. JOEL POLONEY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of And Motion For Issuance Of A Request For Judicial Assistance (Letter Of Request) Under The Hague Convention On The Taking Of Evidence Abroad In Civil Or Commercial Matters
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 30, 2018, Line 6, PLAINTIFF DAVID KANE'S, Motion For Issuance Of A Request For Judicial Assistance (Letter Of Request) Under The Hague Convention On The Taking Of Evidence Abroad In Civil Or Commercial Matters. Plaintiff David Kane's motion for issuance of a request for judicial assistance (letter of request) under the Hague Convention on the taking of evidence abroad in civil or commercial matters is granted. Regardless of who bears the burden, Mr. Kane has shown good cause for the taking of the oral examinations of Nadia Ferriday and Keith McGuinn in the United Kingdom. Defendant Joel Poloney's concerns about the cost of the examinations can be dealt with by his participating in the examinations by video conference or similar technology. The parties are required to meet and confer to ensure that, if desired, Mr. Poloney's counsel is able to participate in the examinations by video conference or similar technology. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is confirmed, the court will sign the proposed letter of judicial assistance submitted with the moving papers. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: ARROWOOD INDEMNITY COMPANY VS. MELISSA L GRIFFIN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Plaintiffs Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment, Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication Of The Issues
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 30, 2018, Line 9, PLAINTIFF ARROWOOD INDEMNITY COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication Of The Issues. Plaintiff Arrowood Indemnity Company's motion for summary judgment or alternatively summary adjudication is denied. There is a triable issue whether the parties settled the dispute and defendant Melissa Griffin fully performed the terms of the settlement. (Griffin Dec. pars. 21-25). Ms. Griffin's declaration states that she and Arrowood's agent (Regional Adjustment Bureau) agreed to settle the matter for $5,600 and she made all of the required payments. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Ms. Griffin is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: ILANA DIAMOND VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Interrogatory Responses
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 30, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF ILANA DIAMOND's Motion To Compel Further Interrogatory Responses. Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to compel further responses is granted as to: General Interrogatory No. 12.1 (because witness addresses are not, absent special circumstances not present here, confidential and any privacy interest in said addresses is outweighed by plaintiff's need for the information and the countervailing interest in the discovery of the truth of matters in litigation); General Interrogatory No. 12.2 (because the preliminary showing required by the Coito decision has not been made and no attorney-client relationship has been demonstrated. Puerto is not to the contrary. Witness identification and location is basic civil discovery. Moreover, the interrogatory does not seek the substance of any communication nor identify any attorney-client relationship.); Employment Interrogatory No. 209.2 (because other cases against Uber may lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Beyda is not to the contrary; it concerns admissibility of evidence, not discovery thereof); Special Interrogatories Nos. 2-5 (because the inquiries turn on the propriety of the independent contractor classification, not on who first adopted the classification); Special Interrogatory No. 15 (same reason as General interrogatory No. 12.1 supra); Special Interrogatory No. 16 (same reason as General Interrogatory No. 12.2. supra); Special Interrogatory No. 19 (facts are requested, not legal arguments). Counsel to plaintiff shall submit a proposed order in Word format consistent with the foregoing by email to jameshfleming@gmail.com prior to the hearing. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jameshfleming@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HOWARD CLARK VS. NUTTZO LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 30, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT NUTTZO LLC.'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant NuttZo LLC's anti-SLAPP motion to strike both causes of action alleged in the complaint filed by plaintiff Howard Clark is denied as to both causes of action. The commercial speech exemption per CCP 425.17(c) applies to Mr. Clark's UCL and CLRA claims and neither of those claims arise from protected speech in the anti-SLAPP statute. Mr. Clark has shown that 1) NuttZo is a corporation primarily in the business of selling nut and seed butter products at issue in this case; 2) the alleged violations of CLRA and UCL arise from NuttZo's "no added sugar" label, which is a statement of fact regarding NuttZo's product; 3) NuttZo's label is made to promote and sell the product; and 4) the label is intended for potential consumers of the product. The "no added sugar" statement is not protected per CCP 425.16(e)(2) because it was not made in connection with an issue under consideration or review by the FDA or any other official body. The statement is also not protected under CCP 425.16(e)(4) because Mr. Clark's claims do not concern public matters of health and nutrition, but rather alleged mislabeling in violation of applicable law. Mr. Clark's request for attorneys' cost and fees is denied. Mr. Clark has not shown that Nuttzo's motion is frivolous. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Clark is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: VERONICA MCCLUSKEY VS. AIRBNB, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 30, 2018, Line 14, DEFENDANT AIRBNB, INC.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration. Defendant Airbnb, Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration is granted and this case is stayed pending the conclusion of arbitration proceedings regarding the claims alleged in the complaint filed by plaintiff Veronica McCluskey. After review of the parties' supplemental briefs, the court re-affirms the tentative ruling issued prior to the April 17, 2018 hearing with the modification that the delegation clause in both versions 6 and 7 require that an arbitrator, not this court, decide whether the parties' arbitration agreement is enforceable. Airbnb has shown that Ms. McCluskey assented to the Terms of Service, including the arbitration language therein, because the sign up screen adequately informed Ms. McCluskey that, by clicking to sign up, she was agreeing to the Terms of Service. Under both the Federal Arbitration Act and the California Arbitration Act, parties may delegate issues of arbitrability to the arbitrator, including disputes regarding the scope and the enforceability of the arbitration agreement and whether the arbitration agreement is unconscionable. (Aanderdund v, Superior Court (2017) 13 Cal. App. 5th 880, 892; Tiri v. Lucky Chances, Inc. (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 231, 240). The arbitration language of both versions 6 and 7 of the Terms and Services is clear and unmistakable evidence of the parties' intent to delegate the issue of enforceability of the arbitration agreement to the arbitrator. Unlike Baker v. Osborne Development Corp. (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 884, 891, there is no provision in the Terms of Service that refers to both the court and the arbitrator as having jurisdiction to rule on the enforceability of clauses of the agreement. The reference to state and federal courts in California in the section entitled "Controlling Law and Jurisdiction" pertains to the carve-out for injunctive and equitable relief and, per Aanderud, 13 Cal.App.5th at 893-894, this does create any ambiguity as to whether arbitrability has been delegated to the arbitrator. Ms. McCluskey does not appear to contend that the delegation clause is unconscionable separate and apart from the remainder of the arbitration agreement, which would be an issue to be decided by the court rather than the arbitrator. But even if she did, she does not identify anything substantively unconscionable about the delegation clause, so that clause is valid and enforceable per the unconscionability doctrine. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Airbnb is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)
